## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content: fb1429dc jjb:1011-36405368:₢A-AAm:n: L3c of implement-jurisdiction: garrison ceremony for shell-letters b/c/w. bujb_jurisdiction.sh gains the operational helpers and the 6-step orchestrator. zbujb_assert_shell_letter validates b/c/w; zbujb_garrison_assert_platform enforces per-letter platform invariant (b∈{bubep_linux,bubep_mac}, c/w=bubep_windows). zbujb_workload_home echoes /home/wlu (linux/cygwin/wsl) or /Users/wlu (mac). zbujb_admin_exec is the central remote-dispatch primitive: reads bash from stdin, wraps in shell-letter-correct invoker (`bash -s` for b; `C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login -s` for c; `wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -s` for w), and ssh's it to BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER@BURN_HOST under BatchMode/IdentitiesOnly/StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new/ConnectTimeout=15. The 6 step helpers (zbujb_garrison_step{1..6}_*) implement the BUSJG{B,C,W} contract: step1 admin SSH probe with platform-aware failure pointer (fenestrate handbook for Windows, ssh-copy-id for Linux/Mac); step2 destroy via userdel -r (b/w) or net.exe user /delete + cygdrive purge (c); step3 create via useradd --create-home + passwd --lock (b-linux/w), sysadminctl + dscl (b-mac, deferred to in-environment refinement), or net.exe user /add /passwordreq:no + mkpasswd -l + chown (c); step4 place authorized_keys with locally-composed `<command_directive> <pubkey>` line (pubkey from ssh-keygen -y on workload privkey, command_directive from bujb_command_for); step5 plant workload privkey via base64-embed-in-heredoc + base64 -d on remote + install/cp with 0600 + chown to workload; step6 round-trip validate via direct workload SSH `true` exit 0. bujb_garrison(LETTER) is the public orchestrator — asserts ZBUJB_RESOLVED first, calls platform invariant, runs steps in order. bujb_cli.sh gets thin wrappers bujb_garrison_{bash,cygwin,wsl} that fold in BUZ_FOLIO check + resolve_investiture + bujb_garrison b/c/w. Zipper enrolls BUWZ_JP_GARRISON_{BASH,CYGWIN,WSL} → buw-jpG{b,c,w}, channel param1. Three tabtargets minted (logged — capture trace useful when the ceremony runs). bash -n green; tt/buw-st green (5 fixtures, 28 cases); buw-jpGb smoke routes through to bujb_garrison_bash and dies cleanly on missing investiture. End-to-end testing on real Windows + Linux nodes happens in AAD/E/F.

Found it.

**Standardization commit:** `385589f4` — "Standardize base64 encoding/decoding to openssl enc -base64 across 6 files" (replaced `base64`/`base64 -D` with `openssl enc -base64` in rbgg, rbgp, rbgo, rbfd, rbv, rgbs).

**Regression commit:** `fb1429dc` (Mon May 4 16:07:22 2026, pace ₢A-AAm) — added new raw `base64` calls when introducing `Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Three sites:

- `bujb_jurisdiction.sh:413` — `z_key_b64=$(base64 < "${BUJB_RESOLVED_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE}")` (encode)
- `bujb_jurisdiction.sh:424` — `echo '${z_key_b64}' | base64 -d > "\${ztmp}"` (decode in remote heredoc)
- `bujb_jurisdiction.sh:434` — same decode pattern, second branch

The commit message even names the technique: "step5 plant workload privkey via base64-embed-in-heredoc + base64 -d on remote." So the encode side is local (could become `openssl enc -base64`), but the decode side runs on the remote host inside an SSH'd bash heredoc — that one is constrained by what's available on the remote, not by your local toolchain choice.

Note: `Tools/lmci/bundle.sh` also uses `base64` directly, but that file predates the standardization sweep and lives in a different kit (LMCI), so it's likely out of scope for this question.

content.text:
The natural acceptance test is already wired in: **step 6 of the garrison ceremony itself**.

Look at `bujb_jurisdiction.sh:444-461` — `zbujb_garrison_step6_validate` does an `ssh -i <planted_key> ... true`. If the encode/decode round-trip corrupts a single byte of the privkey, that SSH fails and `buc_die` fires. So the existing live runs (`buw-jpGb` / `buw-jpGc` / `buw-jpGw` against real targets, the AAD/E/F end-to-end work) are the test — they're already a byte-exact round-trip assertion, and you don't need to add anything new.

Two things worth flagging before the swap, though:

1. **Remote toolchain.** Lines 424 and 434 run on the *remote* shell (Linux/Mac/Cygwin/WSL). `openssl enc -base64 -d -A` requires openssl on the remote PATH. Linux/Mac/WSL almost always have it; **base Cygwin does not** — the `openssl` package has to be installed. Worth a one-line preflight in step 5 (or the cygwin garrison handbook) before assuming the decode side will work.

2. **Cheap local pre-check** (optional, before kicking off a real garrison run):
   ```
   openssl enc -base64 -A < "${BUJB_RESOLVED_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE}" \
     | openssl enc -base64 -d -A \
     | cmp - "${BUJB_RESOLVED_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE}"
   ```
   Catches the local encode side and the local form of the pipeline without standing up SSH. If it passes and the remote has openssl, step 6 will pass.

So: no new fixture needed — the existing `buw-jpG{b,c,w}` runs are the test. Just confirm Cygwin has openssl first.

first issue: you pointed out...

ssh -i <planted_key> ... true

... which is a code smell to me: are we suppressing the result of the ssh command?  That seems anti BCG.

content.text:
`true` there isn't a result-suppressor — it's the remote command argument to ssh.

`ssh user@host true` runs `/bin/true` on the *remote* host. SSH requires a command (or `-N` for no-command); `true` is the canonical "do nothing, exit 0" stub. The whole point of the call is to make the remote authenticate, exit cleanly, and let the *local* exit status of `ssh` propagate.

The exit-handling around it is BCG-clean: `|| z_exit=$?` captures the failure (rather than letting `set -e` abort), then `test "${z_exit}" -eq 0 || buc_die ...` asserts and reports. Nothing is being swallowed — the ssh exit code is exactly what's gating the success/failure decision.

If `ssh ... true` succeeds, the planted key authenticated against the new account on the remote, end-to-end. That's the round-trip assertion, and it's a real test result, not a suppressed one.

(Common variants you might see elsewhere: `ssh host exit 0`, `ssh host :`, or `ssh -N -O check`. All same idea.)

Second BBAAX gauntlet attempt failed at rbtdrk_retriever_invest with HTTP 404 'Service account does not exist' on the LIST keys preflight (rbgg_Governor.sh:196-202), immediately after a successful SA create returning a uid. Fix is the two-call shape: rbgu_poll_until_ok against the keys subpath URL absorbs the propagation delay, then the existing rbgu_http_json + rbgu_http_require_ok pair captures the canonical response into ZRBGG_INFIX_LIST_KEYS for downstream USER_MANAGED counting at line 209-210. Docket:
  ## Character
  
  `rbgp_depot_unmake` reads `RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID` (derived from
  `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER` + `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` + literal `"d-"`) instead of
  accepting the target as a parameter. RBSDU spec already declares "depot
  project ID provided as first argument" — implementation diverged.
  Surfaced as spook today: cleaning up leftover canonicals from failed
  gauntlet attempts requires editing `rbrr.env` to retarget, polluting
  regime state for a transient operation.
  
  Compounding bug: nothing prevents unmaking the live RBRR-selected depot.
  
  Decoupling unmake from RBRR exposes a new bug surface — operator can
  route the verb at any GCP project Payor has access to. The structural
  defense (post-BBAAM): displayName anchor `RBGC_DEPOT_DISPLAY_PREFIX`,
  already used by list as the depot-vs-non-depot discriminator. Unmake
  inherits the same consumption-side check.
  
  Adjacent BCG cleanup folds in: the `"d-"` literal in
  `rbdc_DerivedConstants.sh` is not lifted to a tinder constant.
  
  ## Scope
  
  - `rbgp_depot_unmake()` accepts depot project ID as `$1`; all internal
    references switch from `RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID` to the parameter
  - Empty-arg behavior: print buc_tabtarget pointer to `rbw-dl` for
    candidate discovery, then `buc_die` non-zero. No auto-routing to
    list. Single responsibility preserved.
  - New refusal step (early, before authenticate): fail-fast if target
    equals `RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID`; diagnostic names recovery path
    (rename moniker or `rbw-MZ` before unmake)
  - Validate-Target-Depot gains displayName check: response's
    `.displayName` must start with `RBGC_DEPOT_DISPLAY_PREFIX`. Refuse
    otherwise — diagnostic surfaces the mismatch and points operator at
    `rbw-dl` for the candidate list. Symmetric with how list discriminates.
  - Zipper enrollment for `RBZ_UNMAKE_DEPOT`: channel `""` → `"param1"`
  - Tabtarget stays single-file (no imprint variant); param flows through
  - RBSDU spec adds: live-disqualify step (cheap, pre-authenticate),
    empty-arg refusal language, displayName discriminator sub-bullet
    inside Validate-Target-Depot. Existing argument-from-first-position
    language already correct.
  - Lift `"d-"` to `RBGC_depot_project_infix` tinder constant in
    `rbgc_Constants.sh` (top-of-module, lower_snake name per BCG)
  - `rbdc_DerivedConstants.sh` rewrites the `RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID`
    derivation to reference the tinder constant
  
  ## Constraints
  
  - BCG tinder discipline: `RBGC_depot_project_infix` is pure string
    literal, lowercase name part, top-of-module placement immediately
    after sourced-guard, no runtime dependency
  - Spec voice vs implementation voice: RBSDU edits reference
    `{rbrr_*}` / `{rbgc_*}` linked terms (e.g., live-depot derivation
    spelled in terms of RBRR fields + the new infix linked term);
    implementation function compares against the named
    `RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID` constant directly. Single source of truth in
    each voice.
  - Refusal ordering, cheapest first:
    1. Empty-arg refusal (zero traffic)
    2. Live-disqualify (zero traffic, local string compare)
    3. Authenticate
    4. projects.get + state=ACTIVE + displayName discriminator
  - Param shape: full project ID (e.g., `canc-d-canest2100000`), not
    moniker — matches what `rbw-dl` displays and what GCP error messages
    surface
  - `RBGC_DEPOT_DISPLAY_PREFIX` consumed as-is (currently kindle-cased
    SCREAMING despite being pure literal — pre-existing BCG drift, NOT
    in scope to relift this pace)
  
  ## Cross-pace dependencies
  
  - BBAAX (rbw-tP-first-run) does not block on this — today's manual
    rbrr.env-edit dance is acceptable for one cleanup pass — but the
    gauntlet will produce more leftovers, so this lands first
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Formation helper function (e.g., `rbgp_depot_project_id_for_moniker`)
    — speculative until a second call site needs the rule; current scope
    has only `RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID` as the consumer
  - Lifting `RBGC_DEPOT_DISPLAY_PREFIX` from kindle to tinder — adjacent
    BCG cleanup, separate pace if/when
  - Symmetric refactor of other depot operations (levy still kindles
    from RBRR, which is correct since levy IS the act of selecting/
    creating the live depot)
  - Bulk-unmake-leftovers tool — separate pace if the need recurs

# jjezs_slate base64-to-openssl-sweep

## Character

Mechanical sweep — convert any raw `base64` invocation in BUK/RBK shell to
the openssl-standardized form established by commit 385589f4. The
jurisdiction garrison ceremony reintroduced raw calls; potentially others
have leaked since.

## Scope

- Rescan `Tools/buk` and `Tools/rbk` for raw `base64` invocations — do
  not trust prior line numbers, files and call sites may have moved.
  Recipe: `grep -rn '\bbase64\b' Tools/{buk,rbk} --include='*.sh'`,
  filter out `openssl ... base64`, jq's `@base64`, and comment-only
  occurrences.
- Replace encode form: `base64 < FILE` → `openssl enc -base64 -A < FILE`
- Replace decode form: `base64 -d` → `openssl enc -base64 -d -A`
- Decode sites inside SSH heredocs run on the remote shell — same
  substitution applies; remote toolchain is presumed sufficient
  (cygwin/wsl/linux/mac all have openssl available).

## Constraints

- Acceptance for the jurisdiction sites is the existing live garrison
  ceremony — `buw-jpG{b,c,w}` step 6 `ssh ... true` already round-trips
  the planted privkey byte-exact; corrupted decode fails SSH auth.
- No new test fixture needed.
- `-A` flag suppresses line-wrapping. Use it uniformly on encode and
  decode so single-line embedding in heredocs stays valid.

## Out of scope

- `Tools/lmci/bundle.sh` — different kit, predates the sweep
- jq `@base64` filter — internal to jq, not a bash dependency
- Cygwin openssl preflight check — presumed available per operator
